stories.headline: Trump says US-Iran deal will be signed Sunday in Geneva; Iran says no signing Sunday as uranium standoff clouds terms
stories.summary: Trump said the US-Iran 'Islamabad Declaration' would be signed Sunday June 14 in Geneva by VP Vance and Iran's speaker Qalibaf, reopening the Strait of Hormuz within 30 days. But Iran's foreign ministry said on June 13 it would NOT be signed Sunday — possibly remotely 'in the coming days' — citing US 'instability.' Iran's near-weapons-grade uranium remains unresolved: Trump wants it removed while Tehran says it will keep the stockpile. Israel says it is not a party.

perspectives.keyFraming: A memorandum is close, but Iran has tempered Trump's confident Sunday-signing timeline and the hardest nuclear questions remain unresolved.
perspectives.summary: Trump said the deal was 'scheduled to be signed' Sunday in Geneva (VP Vance and Iran's speaker Qalibaf), waiving oil sanctions, releasing ~$24bn in frozen funds and reopening Hormuz within 30 days. Hours later Iran's foreign ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baghaei said it would not be signed Sunday but could be finalized 'in the coming days,' possibly electronically, citing reservations about US 'instability'; reported terms appeared to favour Tehran.

sources.title: Iran says signing of memorandum of understanding with US 'will not be tomorrow'
U.S. and Iran signal peace deal close as reports suggest terms appear to favour Tehran

perspectives.keyFraming: Israel publicly distances itself while the central dispute — removing Iran's highly enriched uranium — stays unresolved.
perspectives.summary: The Times of Israel reported a senior US official saying the pending deal 'leads to' the US obtaining Iran's enriched material, while Trump conceded the MoU only 'conceptually' addresses the nuclear file. Netanyahu's office said Israel is not a party to the deal, and Iranian sources said Tehran had decided to keep its near-weapons-grade uranium inside the country, defying a core US demand.

sources.title: Iranian sources say regime has decided to keep highly enriched uranium in Iran, defying Trump

perspectives.keyFraming: Tehran insists no deal is final and that any agreement must also end the fighting in Lebanon.
perspectives.summary: Foreign Minister Araghchi, speaking via Iranian state media, said the 'Islamabad Memorandum has never been closer' but is unsigned and could still change, describing a two-stage MoU and urging media to stop treating it as finalized. Iran's foreign ministry earlier called reports of a finalized agreement 'merely speculation' and said any deal must halt hostilities in Lebanon as well; officials reiterated they would not ship near-weapons-grade uranium abroad.

sources.title: Iran says no final decision made on deal Trump hopes could be signed soon
Iran's FM says ceasefire deal with US has 'never been closer'

stories.headline: Massive Russian missile-and-drone barrage on Kyiv and Dnipro kills at least 22 as Ukraine's refinery strikes deepen
stories.summary: A large overnight Russian barrage struck Ukraine, with Kyiv the main target; officials reported at least 22 killed (6 in Kyiv, 16 in Dnipro) and 130+ wounded. Ukraine's air force said Russia launched 73 missiles and 656 drones, intercepting most. The strikes follow Ukraine's deep-strike campaign on Russian refineries, which has caused gasoline shortages across nearly 60 regions. Putin admitted the strikes cause damage while vowing to escalate retaliation.

perspectives.keyFraming: A massive Russian barrage kills 22 as Moscow escalates ahead of the G7, with Ukraine's energy strikes biting.
perspectives.summary: PBS NewsHour reported a massive Russian attack on Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities killed at least 22 people, with officials citing 73 missiles and 656 drones launched overnight and most intercepted, framing the assault as escalation amid stalled diplomacy ahead of the G7 summit in France.

sources.title: Massive Russian attack on Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities kills 22 people, officials say

perspectives.keyFraming: Ukraine's repeated refinery hits are exhausting Russia's capacity to adapt, sparking a nationwide fuel crunch.
perspectives.summary: RFE/RL reported that drone hits on refinery hydrocrackers sparked a fuel crunch across nearly 60 Russian regions as Ukraine pounded Russia's refineries, with Kyiv restriking the same plants to delay repairs and Crimea capping fuel purchases at 20 litres per person.

sources.title: Drone Hits On Hydrocrackers Spark Fuel Crunch, As Ukraine Pounds Russia's Refineries

perspectives.keyFraming: Russia frames intensified strikes as retaliation to deter Ukrainian attacks on its civilian infrastructure.
perspectives.summary: Via TASS, Putin said Russia would increase retaliatory strikes on Ukrainian infrastructure to 'deprive it of any desire' to hit Russian civilian facilities, acknowledging Ukraine's attacks were causing damage while insisting the country recovers quickly.

sources.title: Russia to increase retaliatory strikes on Ukrainian infrastructure facilities — Putin

stories.headline: US government orders Anthropic to suspend worldwide access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models over national-security directive
stories.summary: Anthropic shut off global access to its newly launched Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models on June 12 after a US export-control directive ordered it to block 'any foreign national' from using them, citing a reported jailbreak. The models had launched only three days earlier on June 9, and all other Claude models are unaffected. It appears to be the first government-forced takedown of a publicly deployed frontier AI model.

perspectives.keyFraming: Reporting frames the directive as an unprecedented state intervention in a deployed frontier model, with a foreign-national ban forcing a global shutdown.
perspectives.summary: TechRadar and CNN reported that a June 12 US directive ordered Anthropic to block 'any foreign national' from using Fable 5 and Mythos 5 — whether in the US or abroad — just three days after launch, citing a potential jailbreak. Unable to instantly verify every user's nationality, Anthropic disabled the models for all customers worldwide; outlets describe it as a watershed for AI national-security policy.

sources.title: After a 'potential jailbreak', Anthropic is shutting off access to its Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models under national security orders from the US government
Anthropic suspends all access to Mythos model after US government bans foreign nationals' use

perspectives.keyFraming: Anthropic says the underlying jailbreak was narrow and the surfaced vulnerabilities minor, but is complying with the order.
perspectives.summary: In its own statement, Anthropic said it received only verbal evidence of a narrow, non-universal jailbreak — asking the model to read a codebase and fix flaws — and that the surfaced vulnerabilities were minor and already publicly known. The company said it is complying with the government directive while disputing the severity of the reported issue.

sources.title: Statement on the US government directive to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5
Federal government orders Anthropic to pull Fable 5 and Mythos 5, three days after launch

stories.headline: Wall Street rises Friday on record SpaceX debut and US-Iran deal hopes; S&P 500 ends near a record
stories.summary: US stocks closed higher Friday June 12 as a historic SpaceX IPO and optimism over an imminent US-Iran settlement lifted sentiment. The S&P 500 rose 0.5% to 7,431.46, the Nasdaq added 0.3% to 25,888.84 and the Dow gained 354 points to 51,202.26 — recovering from the prior week's chip-driven selloff that briefly knocked the Dow below 50,000. SpaceX (SPCX) closed at $161 (+19% over its $135 IPO price), having raised a record $75B in the largest IPO in Wall Street history.

perspectives.keyFraming: A relief rally on de-escalation plus a record IPO lifts indices back toward records after the chip rout.
perspectives.summary: CNBC reported indices rose Friday on Iran-deal optimism and the SpaceX debut, with the S&P 500 ending near records after rebounding from the prior week's semiconductor selloff and falling oil easing inflation fears ahead of Warsh's first FOMC.

perspectives.keyFraming: A private-sector triumph: Musk's SpaceX prints the biggest IPO in Wall Street history.
perspectives.summary: CNBC's IPO coverage noted SPCX jumped 19% to close at $161 in a record $75B debut at a roughly $1.77 trillion implied valuation — a marquee win for American capital markets and a counterpoint to the week's chip-driven volatility.

sources.title: SpaceX IPO takeaways: SPCX closes at $161, jumping 19% after record debut
